steps.title: Sum of interior angles in a pentagon
steps.content:
The sum of interior angles in any pentagon is $(5-2) \times 180° = 540°$.

Given $\angle B = 139°$ and $\angle D = 174°$:
$$\angle A + 139° + \angle C + 174° + \angle E = 540°$$$$\angle A + \angle C + \angle E = 227°$$

steps.title: Parallel lines and exterior angle turns
steps.content:
Since $AB \parallel DE$, consider how direction changes as we traverse the pentagon from $B$ to $D$ through $C$. At each vertex, we turn by the exterior angle ($180°$ minus the interior angle):
  • Exterior angle at $B$: $180° - 139° = 41°$
  • Exterior angle at $C$: $180° - \angle C$
  • Exterior angle at $D$: $180° - 174° = 6°$
The total directional change from side $AB$ to side $DE$ is:
$$41° + (180° - \angle C) + 6° = 227° - \angle C$$
For parallel lines with opposite orientations (as required in this configuration), this total change must equal $180°$.

steps.title: Solve for angle C
steps.content:
Setting the total directional change equal to $180°$:
$$227° - \angle C = 180°$$$$\angle C = 47°$$
